question.plain: Compute the dot product a · b

palette.description: Multiply each pair of components and sum

steps.description: Dot product: multiply corresponding components and sum them
8 + 3 = 11  ✓  In attention, this is an attention logit between two token vectors

question.plain: Compute the L2 norm ‖v‖₂

steps.description: L2 norm: square each component and sum under the radical
‖v‖₂ = 5  — norms measure the 'size' of an embedding vector

question.plain: Normalize v to a unit vector v̂

palette.description: The L2 norm of v — used as the divisor

steps.description: First compute the norm: √(9+16) = √25 = 5
Divide each component by the norm — unit vectors have ‖v̂‖ = 1

question.plain: Are u and v orthogonal? Compute u · v

palette.description: The dot product is zero — vectors are orthogonal

steps.description: Expand: multiply each pair of corresponding components
Dot product = 0 means orthogonal — Q and K heads in attention seek this separation

question.plain: Compute cosine similarity cos(θ) = (a·b) / (‖a‖‖b‖)

steps.description: Numerator: a · b = 1
Denominator: ‖a‖ = √2, ‖b‖ = 1
Cosine similarity ≈ 0.707 — used to measure semantic closeness of token embeddings

question.plain: Compute the projection of u onto v: proj_v(u)

steps.description: Compute numerator: u · v = 3
Compute denominator: ‖v‖² = 1
Projection = (3/1)v = 3v = [3,0] — attention projects queries onto key directions

question.plain: Compute A + B

steps.description: Matrix addition is element-wise: add each corresponding pair
Evaluate each entry — residual connections in transformers use matrix addition

question.plain: Compute 3A (scalar multiplication)

palette.description: Scalar multiplier applied to every entry

steps.description: Scalar multiplication distributes to every entry of the matrix
Result: scaling a weight matrix rescales every linear transformation it performs

question.plain: Compute the matrix product AB

palette.description: Top-left: row 1 of A dot col 1 of B

steps.description: Each entry (i,j) = dot product of row i of A with column j of B
Matrix multiplication = the core computation in every neural network layer

question.plain: Compute the matrix-vector product Wx

palette.description: First output: row 1 of W dot x

steps.description: Each output component is a dot product of one row of W with x
Wx = [6, 12] — this is exactly what a linear layer computes (before adding bias)

question.plain: Compute BA and confirm BA ≠ AB (non-commutativity)

palette.description: Top-left of BA: row 1 of B dot col 1 of A

steps.description: Compute BA — the order of weight matrices matters in neural networks
BA ≠ AB — matrix multiplication is not commutative; layer order changes the model

question.plain: Compute (AB)v — first multiply AB, then apply to v

steps.description: First compute AB — composing two linear layers into one matrix
Associativity: (AB)v = A(Bv) — deep networks chain transformations efficiently

question.plain: Apply the 90° rotation matrix R to x

steps.description: 90° rotation maps [1,0] → [0,1] — positional encodings use rotations in RoPE

question.plain: Apply the scaling transformation S to v

palette.description: Scale x by 2 and y by 3

steps.description: Diagonal scaling: each feature dimension scaled independently — like learned gain in normalization

question.plain: Compose: compute T₁T₂ (apply T₂ first, then T₁)

steps.description: Composition = matrix multiplication — stacking layers composes their transformations

question.plain: Find the matrix T from the images of the standard basis vectors

palette.description: Columns of T are the images of the basis vectors

steps.description: A linear map is fully determined by where it sends basis vectors — columns of T are the basis images

question.plain: What geometric transformation does F represent? Compute F[1,0] and F[0,1]

palette.description: F sends e₁=[1,0] to [-1,0] — flips x-axis
F sends e₂=[0,1] to [0,1] — y unchanged

steps.description: x-component negated: F reflects across the y-axis

steps.toLatex: F \text{ is a reflection across the y-axis}
steps.description: y-component unchanged — understanding geometry helps interpret what attention heads learn

question.plain: Is T a linear transformation? Compute T([0,0]) to check

palette.description: T(0) ≠ 0 so T is not linear — it is affine

steps.description: A linear map must send 0 to 0. T(0) = b ≠ 0, so T is not linear

steps.fromLatex: T(\mathbf{0}) \neq \mathbf{0} \Rightarrow T \text{ is affine, not linear}
steps.toLatex: T \text{ is affine (linear + bias). Neural net layers y = Wx + b are affine maps}
steps.description: Every neural network layer y = Wx + b is affine, not strictly linear — the bias shifts the origin

question.plain: Write this system in matrix form Ax = b

steps.description: Coefficients form matrix A — the normal equations X^T Xw = X^T y follow the same Ax=b form

question.plain: Solve by elimination: add the equations

steps.description: Adding equations eliminates y — gives x = 3
Back-substitute x=3 into first equation to get y=1

question.plain: Row-reduce the augmented matrix and identify the solution type

palette.description: After R2 → R2 - ½R1: second row becomes all zeros
Free variable x₂ → infinitely many solutions

steps.description: Row 2 = Row 2 − ½ Row 1 — eliminates the first variable from the second equation

steps.fromLatex: \text{One pivot, one free variable} \Rightarrow \text{infinitely many} \text{ solutions}
steps.description: A rank-deficient system has a solution space — in ML this means the model is under-determined

question.plain: Row-reduce. Does this system have a solution?

steps.description: R2 − 3R1 produces [0 0 | 1] — a pivot in the augmented column signals inconsistency
Inconsistent systems arise when the target vector is outside the column space of A

question.plain: Begin Gaussian elimination: apply R2 ← R2 + (3/2)R1

palette.description: New R2 after adding 3/2 of R1

steps.description: Row elimination: add 3/2 of pivot row to zero out the entry below the pivot

question.plain: Solve by back substitution

steps.description: Back substitution starts from the last equation: x₂ = 6/3 = 2
Substitute x₂=2 into row 1: 2x₁ = 5−2 = 3, so x₁ = 3/2

question.plain: Are v₁ and v₂ linearly independent?

palette.description: Only trivial solution c₁=c₂=0 satisfies c₁v₁+c₂v₂=0
Only the trivial solution exists — they form a basis for ℝ²

steps.description: Set the linear combination equal to zero and solve — only the trivial solution c₁=c₂=0

steps.toLatex: \{\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2\} \text{ are linearly independent — they form the standard basis of } \mathbb{R}^2
steps.description: Linear independence is key: a basis of independent vectors spans the full embedding space

question.plain: Express w = [3, 5] as a linear combination of v₁ and v₂

steps.description: Read off the coordinates: c₁=3, c₂=5

question.plain: What is the span of {v₁, v₂}?

palette.description: v₂ = 2v₁ — linearly dependent, span is a line

steps.description: v₂ is a scalar multiple of v₁ — they are linearly dependent
Dependent vectors collapse the span — a weight matrix with dependent columns has reduced rank

question.plain: Find the null space of A: solve Ax = 0

palette.description: det(A)≠0 so only the trivial solution
Null space is trivial — A is invertible, full rank

steps.description: Non-zero determinant → invertible → only Ax=0 solution is x=0
Trivial null space means A is injective — the transformation loses no information

question.plain: Find the rank of A by identifying how many pivots appear after row reduction

steps.fromLatex: \text{Row reduce: rows 2 and 3 become multiples of row 1} \Rightarrow \text{two zero rows}
steps.description: R2−4R1 and R3−7R1 both yield rows proportional to [1 2 3] — only 2 independent rows
Rank 2 < 3 means A is rank-deficient — LoRA exploits this in large weight matrices

question.plain: Find a basis for the null space of A

palette.description: The null space direction: x₁ = −2x₂, free variable x₂ = 1

steps.description: Free variable x₂ = t, so x₁ = −2t — basis vector is [−2, 1]
Null space dimension = #free variables — indicates how many input directions map to zero output

question.plain: Compute trace(A)

steps.description: Trace = sum of diagonal entries = sum of eigenvalues — used in spectral analysis of weight matrices

question.plain: Compute det(A)

steps.description: 2×2 determinant: det = ad − bc
det ≠ 0 means A is invertible — rank is full, no input direction is collapsed to zero

question.plain: Find A⁻¹ using the 2×2 inverse formula

steps.description: Compute det first — det=1 is convenient, inverse = adjugate
Swap diagonal, negate off-diagonal, divide by det — used conceptually in the normal equations W=(X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty

question.plain: What is rank(A)? (Row reduce to find pivots)

steps.description: Count pivot rows after elimination — row 3 = row1 + row2 (linearly dependent)
Rank-deficient means the column space is a proper subspace — LoRA models this with low-rank updates

question.plain: Compute det(A) by cofactor expansion along row 1

palette.description: Cofactor of a₁₁: det[[1,3],[1,2]] = 2−3 = −1, times 1·(+1) = −1... recalc

steps.description: Cofactor expansion: first minor = det[[1,3],[1,2]] = 2−3 = −1
det(A) = 3 ≠ 0 — A is invertible, full rank 3

question.plain: Find A⁻¹ and verify AA⁻¹ = I

steps.description: Compute det = 10
Divide adjugate by det — matrix inversion underlies the pseudoinverse used in least-squares fitting

question.plain: Verify that u and v are orthogonal

steps.description: Compute the dot product — if it equals zero, vectors are orthogonal
Dot product = 0 → orthogonal — Q/K projections in attention aim for orthogonal feature directions

question.plain: Compute the orthogonal projection of u onto v

steps.description: Numerator: u·v = 4·1+3·0 = 4; denominator: ‖v‖²=1
Projection onto the x-axis = [4,0] — attention computes projections of queries onto key directions

question.plain: Verify these two vectors are orthogonal and compute their norms to normalize them

steps.description: Dot product is 0 — the vectors are orthogonal
Compute the norm before normalizing
Orthonormal basis — QR decomposition and RoPE both rely on orthonormal sets

question.plain: Verify Q is orthogonal: check Q^T Q = I

palette.description: Q^T Q = I — orthogonal matrix confirmed

steps.description: Transpose: swap rows and columns

steps.toLatex: Q^T Q = I \Rightarrow Q \text{ is orthogonal}
steps.description: Q^TQ = I confirms orthogonality — rotation matrices preserve inner products and norms

question.plain: Apply one step of Gram-Schmidt to orthogonalize v₂ against v₁

steps.description: Projection scalar: (v₂·v₁)/‖v₁‖² = (1+0+0)/(1+1+0) = 1/2
Subtract the projection — u₂ is now orthogonal to v₁; Gram-Schmidt underlies QR decomposition

question.plain: Find the orthogonal projection of b onto the column space of A

steps.description: Scalar multiplier = (A^Tb)/(A^TA) = 2/2 = 1
Projection onto column space — least-squares regression finds this exact projection

question.plain: Find the eigenvalues of the diagonal matrix A

steps.description: For diagonal matrices the eigenvalues are the diagonal entries — no computation needed
Diagonal = already diagonalized — eigenvalues control how much each direction is scaled

question.plain: Verify v is an eigenvector of A and find its eigenvalue

steps.description: Apply A to v — result should be a scalar multiple of v
Av = 2v → λ=2. Eigenvectors are the 'principal directions' — unchanged except for scaling

question.plain: Find eigenvalues by solving det(A − λI) = 0

steps.description: Characteristic polynomial: det(A−λI) = (4−λ)(3−λ) − 1·2
Factor: (λ−5)(λ−2)=0 → eigenvalues 5 and 2 — eigenvalues of Hessian determine loss landscape curvature

question.plain: Find the eigenvector for λ = 5 by solving (A − 5I)v = 0

steps.description: Subtract 5 from diagonal entries to form A − λI
Set v₁=1 (free), v₂=1 — eigenvectors define the natural axes of the transformation

question.plain: Diagonalize A: confirm A = PDP⁻¹ by identifying D

steps.description: D has eigenvalues on the diagonal — diagonalization reveals the natural coordinate system of A

question.plain: Find the eigenvalues of A. Is A diagonalizable?

palette.description: Only one independent eigenvector — defective matrix

steps.description: Characteristic polynomial: (1−λ)² = 0 — repeated eigenvalue
Only one independent eigenvector found — eigenspace dimension < algebraic multiplicity

steps.toLatex: A \text{ is a Jordan block — not diagonalizable}
steps.description: Defective matrices can't be diagonalized — understanding this avoids pitfalls in spectral methods

question.plain: Given the singular value matrix Σ, what is the rank of the original matrix?

steps.description: Rank = number of non-zero singular values — SVD makes rank transparent

question.plain: Write the SVD of this diagonal matrix (U, Σ, V^T)

palette.description: Σ = A for a diagonal positive matrix (U=V=I)

steps.description: Diagonal positive matrix: singular values = diagonal entries, U=V=I — simplest possible SVD

question.plain: Compute the best rank-1 approximation Â₁ = σ₁ u₁ v₁ᵀ

palette.description: σ₁ = 5 is the largest singular value

steps.description: Rank-1 term = σ₁ · (outer product of u₁ and v₁ᵀ)
Best rank-1 approximation — LoRA uses exactly this: ΔW = BA is a rank-r outer product sum

question.plain: Find LU decomposition: write A = LU where L is lower triangular with 1s on diagonal

palette.description: L = I since no row operations needed

steps.fromLatex: A \text{ is already upper triangular} \Rightarrow L = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0\\0 & 1\end{bmatrix}
steps.description: No elimination steps needed → L = I
LU = I·A = A — LU decomposition enables efficient solving of multiple right-hand sides

question.plain: Write the full SVD of A (σ₁=3, u₁=[1,0]ᵀ, v₁=[1,0]ᵀ)

palette.description: Σ has σ₁=3 at top-left, all other entries zero

steps.description: For this rank-1 matrix, singular value σ₁=3 captures all the 'energy'
SVD outer product form — LoRA adapts W by learning low-rank factors B·A with r≪min(m,n)

question.plain: What fraction of the Frobenius norm is captured by the rank-1 approximation?

steps.description: Frobenius norm = √(sum of squared singular values)
When top singular values dominate, low-rank compression loses little — the insight behind LoRA

question.plain: Compute the L2 norm and L1 norm of v

steps.description: L2 norm: square-root of sum of squared components
L1 norm: sum of absolute values — promotes sparsity (LASSO regularization)

question.plain: Compute the Frobenius norm ‖A‖_F

steps.description: Frobenius norm: square root of sum of all squared matrix entries
Frobenius norm = L2 norm of vectorized matrix — used in weight decay regularization ‖W‖_F²

question.plain: Apply gradient clipping: if ‖∇‖₂ > c, scale ∇ to have norm c

steps.description: Compute gradient norm: 5 exceeds threshold 1 — must scale down
Gradient clipping: scale to unit norm — prevents exploding gradients during training

question.plain: Why do we scale by 1/√d_k? Compute the scaling factor for d_k = 64

steps.description: √d_k = 8 — without scaling, dot products grow with dimension and softmax saturates
Dividing by √d_k keeps variance of dot products ≈ 1 — prevents softmax from having near-zero gradients

question.plain: Compute mean μ and std σ, then normalize x

steps.description: Compute the mean of x
Compute standard deviation — variance of residuals around the mean
Layer normalization stabilizes activations — each token vector is centered and scaled before the next layer

question.plain: Compute the spectral norm ‖A‖₂ (= largest singular value)

palette.description: σ₁ = max diagonal entry = 3 (for diagonal matrices)

steps.description: For diagonal matrices, singular values = absolute diagonal values in decreasing order
Spectral norm = largest singular value — controls Lipschitz constant; used in spectral normalization of GAN discriminators
